pattern: Floyd-Warshall computes all-pairs shortest paths in O(n^3); then a linear scan counts reachable neighbors per city and picks the city with the fewest, preferring the highest-numbered city on ties.
hints: With n up to 100, an O(n^3) all-pairs shortest path algorithm is fast enough. Floyd-Warshall iterates over every triple (k, i, j) and relaxes dist[i][j] through intermediate node k.
After running Floyd-Warshall, count for each city how many other cities have a shortest path at most distanceThreshold.

Walk through cities in order and track the minimum neighbor count seen, always updating the answer when you find a count that is less than or equal to the current minimum — this naturally picks the largest index on ties.

edge_inputs.description: example from the problem statement — tie at 2 neighbors, pick highest index
second example — city 0 isolated by threshold
only two cities, one edge — threshold includes it
only two cities, threshold too small — neither reaches the other
threshold of 1 — most cities unreachable, pick highest-index isolate
all cities form a clique with weight 1 — tie-breaking by index

stress.description: dense random graph, n near max, random threshold
